analogy: Think of "Light Reflection and Refraction" through the chapter's own opening images: The Human Eye and the Colourful World 10 CHAPTER You have studied in the previous chapter about refraction of light by lenses. You also studied the nature, position and relative size of images formed by lenses. How can these ideas help us in the study... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 10 CHAPTER, 10.1 THE HUMAN EYE, 10.1.1 Power of Accommodation, – C. CIBBER. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Light Reflection and Refraction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that the Human Eye and the Colourful World 10 CHAPTER You have studied in the previous chapter about refraction of light by lenses.

The text explains that you also studied the nature, position and relative size of images formed by lenses.

The text explains that how can these ideas help us in the study of the human eye?

The text explains that the human eye uses light and enables us to see objects around us.

The text explains that what is the function of the lens in a human eye?

The text explains that how do the lenses used in spectacles correct defects of vision?

The text explains that let us consider these questions in this chapter.

The text explains that we have learnt in the previous chapter about light and some of its properties.

The text explains that in this chapter, we shall use these ideas to study some of the optical phenomena in nature.

The text explains that we shall also discuss about rainbow formation, splitting of white light and blue colour of the sky.

The text explains that 10.1 THE HUMAN EYE The human eye is one of the most valuable and sensitive sense organs.

The text explains that it enables us to see the wonderful world and the colours around us.

The text explains that on closing the eyes, we can identify objects to some extent by their smell, taste, sound they make or by touch.

The text explains that it is, however, impossible to identify colours while closing the eyes.

The text explains that thus, of all the sense organs, the human eye is the most significant one as it enables us to see the beautiful, colourful world around us.

The text explains that its lens system forms an image on a light-sensitive screen called the retina.

The text explains that we find a structure called iris behind the cornea.

The text explains that the brain interprets these signals, and finally, processes the information so that we perceive objects as they are.

The text explains that science 164 Think it over You talk of wondrous things you see, You say the sun shines bright; I feel him warm, but how can he Or make it day or night? – C.

The text explains that it is a simple process and does not lead to any disfigurement. n Persons who were infected with or died because of AIDS, Hepatitis B or C, rabies, acute leukaemia, tetanus, cholera, meningitis or encephalitis cannot donate eyes.

The text explains that the peculiar shape of the prism makes the emergent ray bend at an angle to the direction of the incident ray.

The text explains that before we take up this question, we shall first go back to the refraction of light through a prism.

The text explains that a rainbow is always formed in a direction opposite to that of the Sun.

The text explains that science 168 Figure 10.10 Figure 10.10 Figure 10.10 Figure 10.10 Figure 10.10 Atmospheric refraction effects at sunrise and sunset 10.5 ATMOSPHERIC REFRACTION You might have observed the apparent random wavering or flickering of objects seen through a turbulent stream of hot air rising above a fire or a radiator.

The text explains that this wavering is thus an effect of atmospheric refraction (refraction of light by the earth’s atmosphere) on a small scale in our local environment.

The text explains that the starlight, on entering the earth’s atmosphere, undergoes refraction continuously before it reaches the earth.

The text explains that further, this apparent position of the star is not stationary, but keeps on changing slightly, since the physical conditions of the earth’s atmosphere are not stationary, as was the case in the previous paragraph.

The text explains that since the stars are very distant, they approximate point-sized sources of light.

The text explains that as the path of rays of light coming from the star goes on varying slightly, the apparent position of the star fluctuates and the amount of starlight entering the eye flickers – the star sometimes appears brighter, and at some other time, fainter, which is the twinkling effect.

The text explains that the planets are much closer to the earth, and are thus seen as extended sources.

The text explains that if we consider a planet as a collection of a large number of point-sized sources of light, the total variation in the amount of light entering our eye from all the individual point-sized sources will average out to zero, thereby nullifying the twinkling effect.

The text explains that 10.6.1 Tyndall Effect The earth’s atmosphere is a heterogeneous mixture of minute particles.

The text explains that very fine particles scatter mainly blue light while particles of larger size scatter light of longer wavelengths.

The text explains that the molecules of air and other fine particles in the atmosphere have size smaller than the wavelength of visible light.

The text explains that these are more effective in scattering light of shorter wavelengths at the blue end than light of longer wavelengths at the red end.

The text explains that the red light has a wavelength about 1.8 times greater than blue light.

The text explains that thus, when sunlight passes through the atmosphere, the fine particles in air scatter the blue colour (shorter wavelengths) more strongly than red.

The text explains that if the earth had no atmosphere, there would not have been any scattering.

The text explains that what is the focal length of the lens required for correcting (i) distant vision, and (ii) near vision?

The text explains that the far point of a myopic person is 80 cm in front of the eye.

The text explains that what is the nature and power of the lens required to correct the problem?

The text explains that make a diagram to show how hypermetropia is corrected.

The text explains that the near point of a hypermetropic eye is 1 m.

The text explains that what is the power of the lens required to correct this defect?

The text explains that assume that the near point of the normal eye is 25 cm.

The text explains that why is a normal eye not able to see clearly the objects placed closer than 25 cm?

The text explains that what happens to the image distance in the eye when we increase the distance of an object from the eye?

The text explains that why does the sky appear dark instead of blue to an astronaut?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 10: Light Reflection and Refraction

prelims_traps: Do not treat 10 CHAPTER and 10.1 THE HUMAN EYE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Light Reflection and Refraction; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 10 CHAPTER and 10.1 THE HUMAN EYE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
